turns.content: I'm reading a tutorial that says I can't use traits with async functions, but then it shows something called async_trait. What is this thing and why do we need it?

That's a great question because you're running into a fundamental limitation of Rust right now. In stable Rust, traits cannot return Future types directly, which means if you define an async function inside a trait like my_trait::async_method, the compiler will error out saying it can't infer the concrete Future type. The crate async-trait solves this by generating a wrapper that turns your method into a proper Future at compile time. It's essentially a macro that replaces your async keyword with code that constructs a Future instance compatible with Rust's trait system.
So I just need to add the import and write async before my function? But what happens if I don't use it?

Exactly, you would import async_trait from the crate as shown in most tutorials. Without it, your trait method trying to be async will simply fail compilation because Rust requires every type in a trait impl to have concrete sizes and lifetimes that can be resolved statically. The generated Future produced by async-trait has all the necessary metadata baked into its definition so the compiler knows exactly what you're working with.
Ah, I see! But wait, isn't this just magic? Why can't native Rust handle this better?

That's a very fair point about why we need the crate. Native async fn in traits was stabilized recently but it requires specific syntax and doesn't support all features like supertraits or dynamic dispatch without extra complexity. The crate acts as an intermediary that provides better ergonomics while still relying on native async capabilities under the hood.
Okay, so I'm using it now but I notice the generated code looks different from what I wrote. What's going on with those extra lines?

Those are the macro expansion artifacts that you won't see unless you enable verbose compilation flags. The crate generates a concrete Future type for your method and attaches it to the trait impl. This allows your trait methods to be called asynchronously without breaking Rust's type system requirements.
Does this make my code slower because of all that extra generation?
Not really, the generated Future is just a normal type. The real performance considerations come from how you actually use async operations in your code rather than whether you used the crate or not.
Right, but I'm worried about memory because every time I call an async method it creates a new Future object. Is that bad?

That's actually the right intuition to have. Each async function call does create a Future instance on the stack or heap depending on context, but modern Rust compilers optimize this well enough for most applications.
I'm confused about ownership too since I'm new to Rust. Can you explain how async_trait handles references?

async-trait automatically manages lifetimes and references for you, which is exactly why it exists - to make writing async traits ergonomic without worrying about the borrow checker errors that normally plague trait methods.
Wait, I thought async functions were faster because they don't block? But if I'm creating new objects every time, doesn't that slow things down?

You're touching on a crucial distinction. Async operations are designed to be non-blocking by suspending execution until work completes rather than actually blocking the thread. The object creation cost is minimal compared to the benefit of not waiting for I/O operations.
I understand now but can you give me a concrete example where async_trait is better than regular traits?

Sure, imagine you have a database connection pool. With regular traits you couldn't easily add async methods to your interface without breaking compilation. But with async-trait you can define an interface that allows all your workers to perform concurrent database queries seamlessly.
That makes sense but what if I want to use the crate in a larger project? Are there any gotchas?
The main consideration is that async-trait adds some overhead to your build process since it needs to generate code, and you should be aware that not all Rust features work perfectly with it yet.
I'm still worried about the learning curve though. How do I know when to use this instead of just regular async functions?

Use it whenever you need to define reusable patterns across multiple components that all perform asynchronous operations. It's especially useful in service layers where you want consistent behavior throughout your codebase.

I'm trying to write a tool that sends HTTP requests, but I keep getting type errors when defining traits. My trait definition looks like this: pub trait RequestHandler where Self: Send + Sync { async fn execute(&self) -> Result<(), Error>; } and Rust is complaining about the missing Future return type.

You're encountering a common issue with stable Rust traits. When you define an async method in a trait without the async-trait crate, the compiler expects the method to return a concrete Future type like Box<dyn Future> or Pin<Box<dyn Future>> instead of just using 'async fn'. The async-trait crate automatically adds the necessary generic Future bounds for you.

That makes sense, but I'm wondering about performance implications. Since async-trait generates a Future wrapper around my actual function, is there overhead compared to using native async fn in traits?

There is some overhead because async-trait manually implements the future and handles the Send + Sync bounds at compile time. Native async fn in traits (available since Rust 1.70) avoids this wrapper entirely but requires more manual boilerplate to add the Future trait bound yourself.
I see, so if I want to avoid that extra indirection and keep my code cleaner, should I just use native async fn in traits? Or is async-trait still worth it for most projects?

For most projects, async-trait is absolutely worth it because it eliminates the boilerplate of manually managing Future types and trait bounds. It makes your trait definitions look exactly like synchronous traits but behave asynchronously.
Okay, I added the derive macro to my trait and it compiles now. But I'm seeing a warning about orphan rules when I try to use this in my library crate.

The orphan rule warning happens because async-trait generates an impl block for the Future type that might conflict with your existing implementations. You typically need to ensure your trait doesn't have conflicting generic bounds or consider using a different approach if this persists.
Actually, I think my issue is that I'm trying to use async-trait on a trait that's already implemented by another crate. Can you explain how the orphan rules interact with third-party traits?
When using async-trait on an existing trait from another crate, you generally cannot add new methods because of the orphan rule restrictions in Rust's type system.
So I can only extend traits I define myself? What if I want to wrap a third-party async trait with my own logic?
In that case, you should create your own wrapper type that implements the third-party trait's methods through delegation rather than trying to modify the original trait definition.
Got it. But I'm still confused about how async-trait handles the Future generation internally. Does it create a distinct type each time or reuse something?

The generated Future type is specific to your trait and method signature combination, but since it's generic over Self, reusing the same trait implementation across different concrete types avoids unnecessary duplication.
I appreciate the clarification. One more thing I'm worried about: what happens if I remove async-trait and implement this manually with the proper Future bounds?
If you remove async-trait, you'll need to explicitly add <Self as Future>::await or use Pin<&mut Self> in your implementation, which adds significant boilerplate compared to using derive.

I have a service that needs to handle concurrent database requests, and I'm torn between using native async functions in traits versus the async-trait crate. Imagine I define a trait with two methods, one that returns a String and another that takes an i32 parameter and returns a Future. If I use native async fn, both methods need explicit type annotations because the compiler can't infer the Future type automatically. But if I use serde_json::Value to serialize my data structures before passing them to the trait, does that help with performance or just clarity?

Using native async fn in traits is cleaner because it removes the need for manual Future type annotations and explicit type parameters on every method. However, when you use serde_json::Value to serialize data before calling these methods, you're actually adding serialization overhead that might negate some of the performance benefits of avoiding macros. The async-trait crate generates a Future type for each trait implementation automatically, which means your code looks like standard traits without needing to manually annotate return types as impl MyTrait for T: Send + Sync.

That makes sense about the automatic Future generation. But I'm worried about the macro overhead when my trait has many methods. If I have a trait with twenty methods that all return Futures, will async-trait compile faster than native async fn? And what happens if I try to use both approaches in the same codebase?

The compilation speed difference is usually negligible for most projects since modern Rust compilers are optimized for macro expansion. More importantly, mixing native async fn and async-trait in the same trait definition causes compile errors because they conflict on how Future types are inferred. You'd need to split your traits into separate definitions or refactor your code to use only one approach per trait.
Okay, so I should pick one. But what about Send + Sync bounds? With async-trait, do I still need to manually add these bounds to every method signature like native async fn requires?

No, async-trait handles the Send and Sync bounds automatically for you. When you define a trait with async methods using async-trait, the generated Future types include these traits by default, so you don't need to repeat them in every method signature. This is one of the main advantages over native async fn where you must explicitly annotate each return type.

I appreciate that simplification. Now I'm wondering about performance testing. If I benchmark my service with both approaches, will async-trait show measurable latency differences compared to native async fn? And what if I use tokio::sync::Mutex instead of std::sync::Mutex inside my async functions?

In most realistic benchmarks, the performance difference between async-trait and native async fn is within noise margins for typical workloads. The real performance bottleneck usually comes from how you manage synchronization primitives like tokio::sync::Mutex versus std::sync::Mutex inside your async functions rather than whether you used macros or native syntax.

That's helpful context. But I'm still confused about the generated Future type structure. When async-trait generates code, does it create a separate struct for each trait implementation, or does it use something more generic? And how does this affect my ability to implement traits from different crates?

async-trait uses a generic approach under the hood rather than creating distinct structs per trait. It generates code that looks like standard Rust with automatic Future wrapping, so you can easily implement traits from external crates as long as they support async methods. The generated Future type is compatible with any crate's trait requirements because it follows standard Rust patterns.
I need more concrete details though. Can I use the macro syntax directly in my existing codebase without refactoring? Or do I have to rewrite all my traits to use native async fn instead?

You can add async-trait to your Cargo.toml and gradually replace your existing trait definitions with the macro syntax. You don't need to completely refactor your codebase at once, but you should be aware that native async fn traits require explicit type annotations which might force some changes in how you write your methods.
